content.full_text:
August 1, 1978


Mr. Richard Schmechel
California Labor Federation, COPE
995 Market Street, Suite 310
San Francisco, California 94103

Dear Mr. Schmechel:

This letter will confirm advice given to you concerning the method
by which the statewide Committee on Political Education (C.O.P.E.)
must report pursuant to the Political Reform Act.

You described C.O.P.E.'s activities as follows:

     Each member of a local union which is a California Labor
     Federation affiliate contributes 15¢ through the local
     (the local acts as the agent for the California Labor
     Federation in collecting the 15¢) to support the on-going
     activities of the Federation. Of the 15¢, 10¢ is used
     by the Federation and 5¢ is allocated to COPE. Each
     central labor council affiliate is assessed $2.00 per
     month; $1.00 to the California Labor Federation and $1.00
     to C.O.P.E. Unless special assessments are made, which
     you stated were very unusual, this is the method by which
     C.O.P.E. obtains its funds.

The funds are used to finance:

     1. A standing sub-committee for the purpose of making
        campaign contributions to state candidates.

     2. Contributions to state and local ballot measure
        committees.

     3. Administrative costs including salaries for C.O.P.E.
        employees.

     4. Get-Out-The-Vote drives.

     5. An extensive voter registration program.

Mr. Richard Schmechel                    -2-                        August 1, 1978


     6.  Women's activity groups. These groups are designed
primarily to educate women concerning labor goals
and how to become active in the community as
members of the labor movement.

Additionally, you have stated that C.O.P.E. receives monies for
Voter Registration and Get Out the Vote from the National C.O.P.E.
office in Washington, D.C. and that these monies are disbursed to
various local C.O.P.E.s throughout the state upon request. The
National C.O.P.E. funds are transmitted with a letter stating
that they should not be used for political purposes. Political
purposes may also include, in addition to contributions made to
or on behalf of state or local candidates, expenditures made for
partisan Get Out the Vote and Voter Registration efforts.

You have provided us with a breakdown of approximate percentages
of funds used for the various categories:

                                     ELECTION      NON-ELECTION
                                     YEAR          YEAR

C.O.P.E.'s Standing Sub-Committee     17.4%         5.7%
State and Local Ballot Measures        3.6%         1.8%
Voter Registration                    24.8%         2.8%
Womens Education and Activities        1.4%         3.0%
Administrative Overhead Costs         52.8%        86.7%
                                     100.0%       100.0%

     Presently C.O.P.E. is required to report and has been reporting
all funds received from central labor councils and individual
locals. That is, you are reporting the total amount each local
receives from members (5¢ per member) and the total of the $1.00
per month dues received from each central labor council. Except
for occasional local contributions, the only payments itemized
are lump sum payments made to the standing sub-committee, which
in turn makes contributions to state candidates.

     It was my understanding when I called you on July 5, 1978, and
urged you that you should continue to report as you have been
[illegible] of National C.O.P.E. funds was being reported to
[illegible] As a result of a subsequent conversation with
[illegible] Division, however, we have determined
[illegible] nor the local C.O.P.E.s report
[illegible] though the National funds are
[illegible] State C.O.P.E. advising that
[illegible] it is our understand-
[illegible] the funds are

r opinion that State C.O.P.E.
continue to report all funds
eceived by the State
ransferred to the local
.P.E., listing the local

Mr. Richard Schmechel                                          August 1, 1978
                              -2-

C.O.P.E. as an intermediary for the expenditure. Alternatively,
the transfer of these funds could be treated as contributions to
the local C.O.P.E.'s and the receipt and expenditure of the funds
reported by the local C.O.P.E.s if and when they have filing
obligations. Either scheme would be acceptable as long as there
is complete disclosure of funds used for political purposes.

I trust this letter will provide sufficient guidance to allow
accurate reporting under the law. If, however, you desire addi-
tional information or wish to appeal the advice of the staff,
please do not hesitate to contact me.

                                          Very truly yours,


                                          Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
                                          Technical Assistance Program
                                          Coordinator

RSK:bw

cc: John Middleton
    Franchise Tax Board

State of California

Memorandum

To     : Opinion Request Meeting Participants          Date  :  June 26, 1978


From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Gina Siciliano-Kutchins

Subject: Follow-up Information concerning C.O.P.E.


In March 1978, Alan was requested to get a breakdown from COPE
outlining their activity by category in order to determine whether
they qualify as a multi-purpose or primary purpose committee.
Attached is the percentage breakdown they have provided and other
pertinent correspondence.

(Mr. Schmechel has been ill which resulted in a delay in receiving
the information.)

CALIFORNIA LABOR FEDERATION, AFL-CIO

JOHN F. HENNING
Executive
Secretary-Treasurer

995 MARKET STREET, SUITE 310
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94103
415 9434285

June 21, 1978

ALBIN J. GRUHN
President

GENERAL
VICE PRESIDENT
Manuel Dias

VICE PRESIDENTS
Geographical
District No. 1
Max J. Oslo
District No. 2
M. R. Callahan
Ray S. Mendoza
District No. 3
Joseph Finty
George Williamson
Jerry P. Cremins
Alfred K. Whitehead
Cornelius Wall

Willis P. Simons
District No. 7
C. A. Green
District No. 8
Val Connolly
District No. 9
Herma Weinberger
James R. Rowland
Frank Souza
Wm. G. Dowd
District No. 10
Richard K. Grolix
William Ward
District No. 11
Leo Mitchell
District No. 12
Loretta Riley
District No. 13
Harry Finks
District No. 14
Raymond K. Nelson
District No. 15
Lloyd J. Lea

VICE PRESIDENTS
At Large
James F. McLoughlin
Dina G. Beaumont
William R. Robertson
William Waggoner
Hal Shean
John F. Crowley
Steve Edney
Fred D. Fletcher
Cass Alvin

Mr. Al Herndon
Compliance Representative
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Herndon:

Below is the information requested by you in your letter of March 30, 1978. As you will remember, I informed you by telephone that I did not receive your letter until approximately April 22, 1978.

The approximate percentages for the various categories are listed below:

                                          ELECTION   NON-ELECTION
                                            YEAR        YEAR

C.O.P.E.'s Standing Sub-Committee         17.4%        5.7%
State and Local Ballot Measures            3.6%        1.8%
Voter Registration                        24.8%        2.8%
Womens Education and Activities            1.4%        3.0%
Administrative Overhead Costs             52.8%       86.7%
                                         100.0%      100.0%

The Voter Registration and Get Out the Vote Funds are one and the same.

Monies used for Voter Registration and Get out the Vote are received from the National C.O.P.E. office in Washington, D. C. These monies are disbursed to various local C.O.P.E.s throughout the state at their request.

                                          Sincerely,

                                          [signature]
                                          R. B. Schmechel
                                          Accountant

RBS:mg
ope-3-afl-cio(31)
cc: Mr. Henning
    Mr. Scully
    Mr. Anderson

March 30, 1978


Mr. Richard Schmechel
California Labor Federation, COPE
995 Market Street
San Francisco, California 94103

Dear Mr. Schmechel:

During a recent staff discussion of your request for evaluation of
the method by which the statewide Committee on Political Education
(C.O.P.E.) reports contributions and expenditures on its campaign
statements, it was determined that no decision could be reached
without additional background information. In accordance with that
determination, I am requesting the following information:

1. The approximate percentage of COPE's budget, in both an election
year and a non-election year, devoted to each of the following
activities:

   a. Making contributions to COPE's Standing Sub-committee for
      use in making contributions to state candidates;

   b. making campaign contributions to state and local ballot
      measure committees;

   c. funding COPE's Get-Out-The-Vote drives;

   d. funding COPE's voter registration program;

   e. funding women's education and activity groups; and,

   f. funding COPE's administrative overhead costs.

2. A brief description of how COPE's voter registration program is
   conducted.

Your early attention to this request will be appreciated. If you
have any questions, do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,

Alan Herndon
Compliance Representative

DRAFT
3/8/78

Mr. Richard Schmechel
California Labor Federation, COPE
Market Street
San Francisco, California

Dear Mr. Schmechel:

This letter will confirm advice given to you concerning the method
by which the statewide Committee on Political Education (C.O.P.E.)
must report pursuant to the Political Reform Act.

You described C.O.P.E.'s activities as follows:

     Each member of a local union which is a California Labor
Federation affiliate contributes 15¢ through the local
(the local acts as the agent for the California Labor
Federation in collecting the 15¢) to support the on-going
activities of the Federation. Of the 15¢, 10¢ is used
by the Federation and 5¢ is allocated to COPE. Each
central labor council affiliate is assessed $2.00 per
month; $1.00, to the California Labor Federation and $1.00
to C.O.P.E. Unless special assessments are made, which
you stated were very unusual, this is the method by which
C.O.P.E. obtains its funds.

The funds are used to finance:

     1. A standing sub-committee for the purpose of making campaign
        contributions to state candidates. This sub-committee was
        established at the request of the Commission. Mr. Henning,
        a registered lobbyist, sits on the statewide board of
        C.O.P.E. which makes decisions concerning the disbursement
        of all C.O.P.E. funds except contributions made from the
        standing sub-committee's account.

2.  Contribution to state and local ballot measure committees.

3.  Administrative overhead costs including salaries for C.O.P.E.
    employees.

4.  Get-Out-The-Vote drives.

5.  An extensive voter registration program.

6.  Women's activity groups.  These groups are designed primarily
    to educate women concerning labor goals and how to become
    active in the community as members of the labor movement.

Presently COPE is required to report and has been reporting all funds
received from central labor councils and individual locals.  That is,
you are reporting the total amount each local receives from members
(5¢ per member) and the total of the $1.00 per month dues received
from each central labor council.  Except for occasional local contri-
butions, the only payments itemized are lump sum payments made to
the standing sub-committee which in turn makes contributions to
state candidates.

Based on the above information, it is our opinion that COPE is a
multi-purpose committee.  As a multi-purpose committee, only payments
received by it which are earmarked for making contributions or expen-
ditures are reportable contributions within the meaning of the
Political Reform Act (see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(d)).  A
payment is "earmarked" when at the time of making the payment the
donor knows or has reason to know that the payment or funds with
which the payment will be commingled will be used to make contribu-
tions or expenditures.

A reliable method of determining what portion of a payment received
from an employee represents  a contribution is to allocate on the

basis of the amount of money contributed or expended for political
purposes or the amount deposited in a special account designated for
use in making contributions.

If CCPE does not know exactly how much it will receive in members'
dues or how much it will contribute or expend for political purposes,
good faith estimates are acceptable for purposes of prorating. Based
on the information provided, it would appear that all funds received
or expended in connection with the activities outlined in numbers
(1) and (2) above should be reported as being made or received for
political purposes. Funds received or expended for activities out-
lined in numbers (3) through (6) should be reported only on a pro-
rated basis, if a portion of the activity is an attempt of influence
the action of voters for or against the nomination or election of
a candidate or the qualification or support or opposition of any
measure on the ballot.

I trust this letter will provide sufficient guidance to allow accurate
reporting under the law. If, however, you desire additional informa-
tion, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Very truly yours,

RSK

cc: John Middleton
    Franchise Tax Board

sections.conclusion: Based on the information provided, it is our opinion that COPE is a multi-purpose committee. As a multi-purpose committee, only payments received by it which are earmarked for making contributions or expenditures are reportable contributions within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. Funds received or expended for activities outlined in numbers (1) and (2) above should be reported as being made or received for political purposes. Funds received or expended for activities outlined in numbers (3) through (6) should be reported only on a pro-rated basis, if a portion of the activity is an attempt to influence the action of voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or the qualification or support or opposition of any measure on the ballot.
sections.question_synthetic: How must the California Labor Federation's Committee on Political Education (C.O.P.E.) report contributions and expenditures under the Political Reform Act, given its multi-purpose structure with funds allocated to both political and non-political activities?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC determined that C.O.P.E. is a multi-purpose committee and must report only payments earmarked for political purposes as reportable contributions. For activities with both political and non-political components, C.O.P.E. should use pro-rated reporting based on good faith estimates if exact figures are unknown.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple dated versions (DRAFT 3/8/78, August 1, 1978, references to 1979 correspondence). Main advice letter clearly identified in draft version with question, opinion, and conclusion sections. Some text degradation/OCR errors present. Document includes follow-up correspondence and internal FPPC notes showing evolution of advice regarding National C.O.P.E. fund reporting. Confidence reduced due to unclear document order and some legibility issues.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How must the California Labor Federation's Committee on Political Education (C.O.P.E.) report contributions and expenditures under the Political Reform Act, given its multi-purpose structure with funds allocated to both political and non-political activities?

CONCLUSION: Based on the information provided, it is our opinion that COPE is a multi-purpose committee. As a multi-purpose committee, only payments received by it which are earmarked for making contributions or expenditures are reportable contributions within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. Funds received or expended for activities outlined in numbers (1) and (2) above should be reported as being made or received for political purposes. Funds received or expended for activities outlined in numbers (3) through (6) should be reported only on a pro-rated basis, if a portion of the activity is an attempt to influence the action of voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or the qualification or support or opposition of any measure on the ballot.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises the California Labor Federation's C.O.P.E. committee on proper reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act, classifying it as a multi-purpose committee and establishing guidelines for determining which contributions and expenditures must be reported based on whether funds are earmarked for political purposes.
